ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Black Pepper Crab. FOODON_03302757 (crab dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crabs as a category; no specific black pepper crab preparation code exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for black pepper crab or equivalent prepared crab dish.

serving.description: 1 medium whole crab (approximately 600–800 g live weight, ~350 g edible cooked meat and shell-on portions)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical medium serving of Black Pepper Crab consists of one whole medium-sized mud crab (approximately 600–800 g live weight), yielding roughly 350 g of shell-on cooked portions served at the table.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139) adapted with black pepper sauce composition from HPB Singapore nutrient reference for pepper crab
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base crab meat values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked crab (blue crab, moist heat, NDB 15139 / FDC 174202). Sauce contribution (black pepper, butter, garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce) estimated from HPB Singapore nutrient data for stir-fried crab dishes and standard recipe proportions. Sodium elevated to reflect sauce. Values represent edible shell-on portion as served. Confidence is medium due to recipe-level estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of shellfish and protein-dominant seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked crab and low-carbohydrate seafood reference values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Pepper Crab in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of cooked crab meat with sauce. Analogous reference: cooked shellfish and protein-dominant seafood dishes consistently show GI ≤ 20 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 13.3) / 100 ≈ 2.0; however, edible carbohydrate fraction of shell-on crab serving is approximately 5.3 g net, giving GL ≈ 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning in the black pepper sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Black Pepper Crab consumption due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g), which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake; a single medium crab serving may deliver 1,500–2,000 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black Pepper Crab has a low glycemic index (estimated 15) and low net carbohydrate content, making it a suitable protein-rich seafood choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sauce may contain added sugars and cornstarch, so portion control of the sauce is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from butter in the black pepper sauce, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids in crab meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Black Pepper Crab in moderation; while crab meat provides beneficial lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, the high sodium content and butter-based sauce contribute saturated fat and excess sodium that may adversely affect blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Crab (黑椒螃蟹) is one of Singapore's most iconic seafood dishes, alongside Chilli Crab, and is a signature preparation of Singaporean Chinese cuisine. The dish features a whole live mud crab (Scylla serrata), typically of medium size (600–800 g live weight), that is cleaned, chopped, and wok-fried at high heat with a rich, aromatic black pepper sauce. The sauce is made from freshly cracked or coarsely ground black pepper, butter, garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and sometimes egg, creating a bold, pungent, and slightly creamy coating that clings to the crab shell and meat. The flavour profile is intensely peppery, savoury, and buttery, with a dry-style sauce that contrasts with the wetter, tomato-based Chilli Crab. The crab meat is sweet, firm, and succulent, absorbing the pepper and butter flavours during wok cooking. Nutritionally, Black Pepper Crab is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, making it glycemically favourable. It is rich in vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus from the crab meat. The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content derived from the sauce ingredients, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish, egg, soy, or gluten allergies. It is typically served as a shared main course at seafood restaurants in Singapore, accompanied by fried mantou (Chinese buns) for dipping into the sauce. The medium size designation refers to the live weight of the crab, typically 600–800 g, yielding approximately 200–250 g of edible crab meat.
common_user_queries: How is Singapore Black Pepper Crab made?
How many calories in Black Pepper Crab?
Is Black Pepper Crab suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between Black Pepper Crab and Chilli Crab?
Is Black Pepper Crab high in sodium?
What allergens are in Black Pepper Crab?
Can I eat Black Pepper Crab if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: shared main course at seafood restaurants

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN nutrient entry exists for Black Pepper Crab as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked crab (FDC 174202) with sauce contribution estimated from standard recipe proportions and HPB Singapore reference data for stir-fried crab dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of the dish. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base crab nutrient data is from an approved primary source and the sauce estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood', which is the correct classification for a crab dish. 2. Nutrient values per 100 g represent the edible shell-on portion as served, including sauce contribution. Edible yield from a medium mud crab is approximately 35–40% of live weight. 3. Sodium_mg of 520 mg per 100 g reflects the sauce-heavy preparation; plain cooked crab meat would be approximately 293 mg per 100 g (USDA). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked crab (FDC 174202) and is not available in the HPB Singapore reference for this dish category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this preparation. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Black Pepper Crab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains butter and the halal certification of the specific restaurant (Bei Jiang Restaurant) is not confirmed; consumers should verify with the restaurant. 8. The cuisine_tags field in the input included 'Noodle', which is incorrect for this dish and has been disregarded. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked crab data and are not confirmed for this specific restaurant preparation.
